structured_choices.data_source: Three years of sensor readings from production environment (36 months). Data includes timestamped records from multiple machine types with raw sensor streams: temperature, pressure, vibration. Organized by machine ID and operational cycle. Source: plant SCADA system and cross-referenced maintenance logs for failure event ground truth.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label (failure/no-failure) derived from maintenance logs. A failure event is defined as any unplanned equipment shutdown requiring corrective maintenance, confirmed in the maintenance record with timestamp. Pre-failure window: sensor sequences are labeled as positive (failure imminent) if they occur within 168 hours (7 days) before a logged failure event; sequences outside this window are labeled negative (normal operation). This window is chosen to align with the plant's preventive maintenance scheduling cycle and gives operators actionable lead time.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split stratified by machine type. Rationale: (1) Temporal split prevents leakage of future failure information into training; (2) stratification by machine type accounts for heterogeneous sensor distributions and failure modes across different equipment classes. Allocation: months 1–24 (24 months) for training; months 25–30 (6 months) for validation; months 31–36 (6 months) for held-out test. Stratification: within each month range, maintain the proportional distribution of machine types observed in the full dataset. This ensures each machine type is represented in all splits and allows separate evaluation per machine type to detect model degradation on underrepresented equipment.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, sequence normalization) are fit on the training set only (months 1–24). Fitted parameters (e.g., mean/std for z-score scaling, imputation statistics) are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents information from validation and test sets influencing the learned preprocessing parameters.
structured_choices.features: Raw sensor features: temperature, pressure, vibration (captured at their native sampling rate). Time-domain features engineered from training data only: (1) rolling statistics (mean, std, min, max) over 1-hour and 24-hour windows for each sensor; (2) first-order differences (rates of change) in each sensor; (3) machine operating state indicators derived from sensor combinations (e.g., idle vs. active vs. overload). Sequence length: 168-hour lookback window (7 days) to match the pre-failure label window and provide sufficient temporal context for RNN. No features encoding the label or future information (e.g., maintenance schedule dates are excluded).
structured_choices.model: Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM-based). Architecture: bidirectional LSTM with 2 hidden layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3 per layer), dense output layer with sigmoid activation for binary classification. Input: 168-hour sequences of 9 engineered features (3 raw sensors × 3 time-domain variations). Hyperparameters tuned on validation set: learning rate (0.0001–0.001), batch size (32–256), L2 regularization (1e-5–1e-3). Training: Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss, early stopping based on validation loss with patience of 10 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression on hand-engineered statistical features. Feature set: same time-domain features as RNN (rolling statistics, rates of change, operating state indicators) flattened into a fixed-length vector (no sequence dimension). Hyperparameters: L2 regularization tuned on validation set using grid search over same penalty range as RNN (1e-5–1e-3). Tuning effort: equivalent to RNN (grid search on validation set). Rationale: logistic regression represents the traditional statistical method referenced in the hypothesis; same feature engineering removes the confound of feature differences and isolates the contribution of RNN architecture and sequence modeling. Both models are given equal computational resources and tuning iterations.
structured_choices.primary_metric: F1 score (harmonic mean of precision and recall). Justification: the plant manager prioritizes both recall (detecting failures before they occur—avoid unexpected downtime) and precision (avoiding false alarms that trigger unnecessary maintenance—avoid operational disruption). F1 balances both objectives. Secondary consideration: the dataset is assumed to have class imbalance (failures are rare events); F1 is more robust to class imbalance than accuracy.
secondary_metrics: Precision: operational cost of false alarms (unnecessary maintenance interventions)
Recall: risk of missed failures (unplanned downtime, safety hazard)
Area Under ROC Curve (AUC): overall discriminative ability across all thresholds
Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC): accounts for class imbalance and all four quadrants of the confusion matrix
Per-machine-type F1: stratified evaluation to detect failure modes specific to equipment classes
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Grid search is performed over the hyperparameter ranges specified above; the model/hyperparameter combination achieving the highest F1 score on the validation set is selected. The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated once, at the end, with the selected model and hyperparameters. No information from the test set is used during training or validation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and not touched during development. After model selection is complete, the best model is evaluated on the test set exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations) of test set predictions. Test results are stratified by machine type to assess whether the RNN generalizes equally well across all equipment classes.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Operational changes (workload adjustments, maintenance practice shifts) are controlled via temporal stratification. Changes within the 24-month training window are expected to be captured by the model; sudden shifts in months 31–36 (test set) would degrade both models equally and would be detected as a drop in test performance, signaling a distribution shift that should trigger model retraining. Per-machine-type stratification and evaluation (secondary metric) controls for heterogeneity in machine-specific failure modes. Maintenance records are cross-checked for data quality: any failures without clear timestamps or multiple conflicting records are excluded (flagged during data curation). To rule out confounding from sensor drift or calibration changes, sensor diagnostics are reviewed for months 31–36; any documented sensor replacement or recalibration events are noted in the test set analysis. The experiment establishes that the RNN achieves higher F1 than logistic regression under the tested operational conditions and machine portfolio; it does not establish causality (the RNN does not 'cause' the improvement) or generalization to new machine types or significantly different operational regimes.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Statistical Baseline for Equipment Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

The experiment uses 36 months of sensor data from the plant's SCADA system, spanning all industrial machinery in the facility. Raw features include temperature, pressure, and vibration readings captured at their native sampling frequency. Ground truth labels are derived from the maintenance logs: a failure event is any unplanned corrective maintenance action with a confirmed timestamp. All records are cross-checked for data quality; entries with missing or conflicting timestamps are excluded during curation.

To provide actionable lead time and align with the plant's maintenance scheduling cycle, a failure event is operationalized as follows: sensor sequences occurring within 168 hours (7 days) before a logged failure are labeled positive (failure imminent); all other sequences are labeled negative (normal operation). This 7-day window is the primary parameter defining the prediction task.

### 2. Split Strategy

A temporal split stratified by machine type is used:

- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (24 months of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (6 months of data)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (6 months of data)

**Rationale for temporal split:** Sensor sequences are inherently time-ordered; a temporal split prevents the model from accessing future failure information during training. This mirrors real-world deployment where the model makes predictions on unseen future time periods.

**Rationale for stratification by machine type:** Machines exhibit heterogeneous failure modes and sensor distributions. Stratifying ensures that each machine type appears proportionally in all three sets, preventing a scenario where rare machine types are absent from validation/test. This enables per-machine-type evaluation to detect whether the RNN generalizes equally well across the equipment portfolio.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features are engineered from the training set only; fitted statistics are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

**Raw features:** Temperature, pressure, vibration (3 sensors).

**Engineered features (9 total):**
- Rolling mean, std, min, and max over 1-hour windows (4 statistics × 3 sensors = 12 features)
- Rolling mean, std, min, and max over 24-hour windows (4 × 3 = 12 features)
- First-order differences (rate of change) for each sensor (3 features)
- Operating state indicator (derived from sensor combinations to flag idle, active, or overload states)

**Sequence construction:** For the RNN, a 168-hour (7-day) lookback window is constructed for each sequence, providing temporal context that aligns with the 7-day pre-failure label window. The sequence is represented as a tensor of shape (168 timesteps, 9 features). For the baseline (logistic regression), the same 9 engineered features are averaged or summarized over the 168-hour window to produce a fixed-length feature vector (9 features).

No features are constructed from future information (e.g., maintenance schedule dates are excluded) or directly encode the label.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**RNN Model:** A bidirectional LSTM with two hidden layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3 per layer), and sigmoid output for binary classification. Training uses the Adam optimizer with binary cross-entropy loss and early stopping (patience: 10 epochs) on validation loss.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the same 9 engineered features (flattened, no sequence dimension). This represents the traditional statistical approach mentioned in the hypothesis.

**Justification:** Both models receive equivalent tuning effort. Hyperparameters for both are tuned via grid search on the validation set over the same ranges (e.g., regularization from 1e-5 to 1e-3, learning rate from 0.0001 to 0.001 for RNN). By using identical feature engineering and tuning methodology, the comparison isolates the contribution of the RNN's sequential modeling capacity from feature quality differences.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 25–30) using grid search. The metric optimized is F1 score. The model and hyperparameter combination achieving the highest validation F1 is selected. The test set is never used during this process.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** F1 score. Rationale: the plant manager seeks both high recall (detecting failures to prevent unplanned downtime) and high precision (avoiding false alarms that trigger unnecessary maintenance). F1 balances these objectives and is robust to class imbalance, which is expected in failure prediction tasks.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision: quantifies the cost of false alarms (unnecessary maintenance).
- Recall: quantifies the risk of missed failures (unplanned downtime).
- Area Under ROC Curve (AUC): overall discriminative ability across decision thresholds.
- Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC): handles class imbalance and provides a single summary statistic.
- Per-machine-type F1: stratified evaluation to detect whether the RNN generalizes uniformly across equipment classes or exhibits degradation on specific machine types.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls

The test set (months 31–36) is held out and untouched during development. After model selection, both the RNN and baseline are evaluated on the test set exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals derived from bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations).

To account for confounding operational changes (workload, maintenance practices), temporal stratification is used: changes within the training window are absorbed by model learning; sudden shifts in months 31–36 would degrade both models and would be detected as a test performance drop, signaling distribution shift. Per-machine-type evaluation detects any machine-specific performance degradation. Sensor diagnostics are reviewed for months 31–36 to flag any recalibration or replacement events that could confound results.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This design establishes that the RNN achieves higher F1 than logistic regression under the tested operational conditions, machine portfolio, and failure definition. It does not establish causality or generalization to new machine types or radically different operational regimes (e.g., seasonal production shifts not captured in the 36-month window).
